mappings.rationale: Directly covers the art of architecture including design elements, building types, and architectural theory — the primary subject of this section.
Covers modern architectural movements and building types, relevant to the section's treatment of architectural history (section A.7.a).
Covers the history of British architecture, relevant to the section's treatment of Western architecture history (section A.7.a).
Directly covers landscape architecture and design, explicitly addressed in section B on garden and landscape design.
Directly covers the history of garden design, relevant to the section's treatment of garden and landscape design (section B).
Directly covers urban design, the third major topic of this section alongside architecture and landscape design.
Relevant to the section's treatment of urban design and residential architecture, including the planning and design of suburban environments.
Covers design principles relevant to architecture, landscape, and urban design — the three subjects of this section.

Covers ancient Egyptian architecture including temples and tombs, relevant to the section's treatment of religious and commemorative architecture (section A.5.b) and non-Western architecture (section A.7.b).
Covers Renaissance architecture as a major period in Western architectural history (section A.7.a).
Covers Renaissance cultural developments including architecture, relevant to Western architectural history.
Covers Christian architectural traditions including churches and cathedrals, relevant to the section's treatment of religious architecture (section A.5.b).
Relevant to understanding the natural landscape context within which garden and landscape design operates (section B).
Covers the properties of building materials including stone, brick, wood, steel, and concrete — materials explicitly listed in section A.2.a.
Relevant to the section's treatment of constructional systems including post and lintel, arch, vault, dome, truss, and framed structures (section A.2.b).
Relevant to the structural and constructional systems discussed in section A.2.b, including load-bearing walls and framed structures.
Provides theoretical context for understanding architecture as an art, relevant to the section's treatment of design elements and principles of composition.
Byzantine architecture, including domed churches and mosques, is a major tradition in Western architectural history.
Islamic architectural traditions including mosques are explicitly listed under religious architecture (section A.5.b), and Islamic architecture is a major non-Western tradition (section A.7.b).
Ancient Greek and Roman architecture including temples are foundational to Western architecture, relevant to section A.7.a.
Victorian architecture is a significant period in Western architectural history, relevant to section A.7.a.
Futurist architecture challenged conventional design, relevant to the section's treatment of modern Western architecture.
Relevant to the section's treatment of garden and landscape design and the relationship between architecture and the natural environment.
Relevant to contemporary architectural design practices and the relationship between building design, materials, and environmental sustainability.
